Here's my analysis of the Strait of Hormuz situation for the last 24 hours.

As I noted yesterday, I was seeing a lot of vessels using the Oman route to exit. This was following the announcement by Oman and IMO. The IRGC retailiated today on a vessel, but even after the attack, I am still seeing transits. It's a lot less, but there are vessels willing to take the risk.

The cost of shipping #containers from China to major ports in Europe and the US continues to climb, with the Drewry Composite Index this week reaching a 21-month high of USD 4,166 per 40-foot box. That marks a 119% increase since late February, just before the Middle East war disrupted key trade routes, lengthened transit times, and raised operating costs across the industry.

The sharpest increases have been seen on the Shanghai to New York and Los Angeles routes, where freight rates have surged by around 160% over the this period. The move has been supported by an accelerating peak season, with shippers front-loading cargo more aggressively in an effort to build buffers against potential geopolitical disruptions and further supply-chain delays.

What makes the rally notable is that it comes despite weak longer-term industry fundamentals, including persistent overcapacity. So, the current surge does not reflect a demand boom in the traditional sense, but rather a risk-driven squeeze on available shipping capacity.

#Gold's break below USD 4,000 points to continued long liquidation from investors as the metal extends its 26% correction from January's record high. The technical breakdown has further undermined sentiment, forcing additional position reductions despite an underlying macro backdrop that has started to improve over the past week.

The main headwinds remain a stronger US dollar and continued ETF outflows, while speculative positioning has already been reduced significantly. At the same time, several of the factors that fuelled the recent correction are beginning to soften. The sharp collapse in crude oil prices is easing inflation concerns, reducing the need for further Fed tightening. That shift is increasingly reflected in Fed funds futures, where expectations for additional rate hikes have faded, while longer-dated Treasury yields have also moved lower.

Taken together, this suggests the fundamental backdrop is becoming less hostile for gold, even if investor sentiment remains fragile. For now, the market needs to see ETF selling stabilise and the dollar lose momentum before bargain hunters regain confidence. Until then, price action is likely to remain driven more by positioning and technicals than by fundamentals.

#Crudeoil: Brent's front-month spread has swung from a $5.50 backwardation in late April to a $0.35 contango today, the widest contango since 2022. It is a remarkable shift that captures the market's transition from pricing the largest supply disruption on record to anticipating a near-term wall of supply. As stranded Gulf barrels gradually return to the market following the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz, prompt availability has improved faster than immediate demand, temporarily easing the physical market. 

While the move into contango signals short-term oversupply, it does not negate the longer-term support from historically low global inventories that will eventually need rebuilding once this initial wave of supply has been absorbed.

#Commodities

Brent #crudeoil trades below USD 73 and WTI below USD 70, extending a four-session decline that has all but erased the war-related risk premium. The reopening of the Strait of Hormuz has created a short-term wave of supply, pushing prices back towards levels that may prove difficult to sustain once the backlog of stranded barrels has cleared and releases from strategic reserves are scaled back. Longer term, however, slower demand growth driven by electrification and fuel switching may continue to cap upside potential. Meanwhile, over the past 11 weeks, combined US commercial and strategic crude inventories have fallen by 135 million barrels to a multi-decade low.

#Gold slumped below USD 4,000 to a seven-month low, pressured by a resurgent dollar and concerns that higher funding costs may yet lie ahead. The technical breakdown and a 29% correction from January's record high continue to weigh on investor sentiment, leaving the near-term outlook challenged despite steady central bank demand providing an important source of underlying support. At the same time, a continued decline in energy prices and lower bond yields may eventually ease pressure on the Federal Reserve to raise rates.

#Silver, meanwhile, tumbled to a low of USD 55.6 and retracing 61.8% of the rally from its 2022 low at USD 17.56. With industrial metals also suffering a setback, silver is once again exposed to both precious and industrial metal weakness, helping drive the XAUXAG ratio higher towards 70.

#Copper broke key support at USD 6.15 on Wednesday, triggering a wave of technical selling and hedge fund long liquidation. As with precious metals, the main catalysts behind the slide to a seven-week low were a stronger dollar and the Fed's recent hawkish stance, both of which have weighed on the outlook for demand. Prices have since staged a modest rebound, but the technical outlook remains fragile below the mentioned level.

Top Stories

Apple Inc. announced price increases for Mac, iPad, home devices, and Vision Pro, with adjustments of up to 20% on multiple models to offset sustained surges in memory and storage chip costs. The price hikes are global. Apple did not raise prices for iPhone, Apple Watch, or AirPods. The company stated it has never witnessed such rapid and substantial increases in component prices and had previously absorbed cost pressures for consumers, but now has no choice but to pass them on.

Hours after Apple announced price hikes for MacBook and iPad, Microsoft also issued a statement that consumers will face higher prices for Xbox gaming consoles due to continued increases in key component costs. Microsoft cited storage and memory prices rising over 2.5 times as the primary driver. Starting August 1, prices for multiple Xbox models will increase. Microsoft also announced it will discontinue sales of the 2TB version of the Xbox Series X launched in 2024.

IBM unveiled the world’s first sub-1-nanometer chip technology, employing a revolutionary 3D “nanostack” architecture that pushes the node to 0.7nm. The new chip doubles transistor density, offering a 50% performance boost or 70% energy efficiency gain compared to 2nm nodes, with mass production expected within five years. This marks the semiconductor industry’s formal entry into the atomic-scale era, surpassing previous physical limits.

Oman’s Foreign Minister Badr stated that future arrangements concerning the Strait of Hormuz will not involve any tolls. Iran estimates that charging for security and environmental services related to the strait could generate $40 billion annually for involved nations. The Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps Navy emphasized that vessels must coordinate passage in advance, and non-compliant ships “will be dealt with.” S&P Global Energy reported that daily traffic through the Strait of Hormuz in June had recovered to approximately 57% of pre-conflict levels.

The Federal Reserve’s preferred inflation gauge was released. Additionally, the final Q1 real GDP growth was revised up to 2.1% annualized, above the prior estimate of 1.6%. MARKET UPDATE — THURSDAY, 25 JUNE 2026

The AI rally has survived its first major inflation test.

Micron delivered extraordinary earnings, semiconductor shares surged globally and Nasdaq futures climbed more than 2%.

US inflation then rose above 4% for the first time in three years—but the monthly increase was slightly softer than feared.

The result is a delicate market balance:

AI profits are accelerating.

The economy remains resilient.

But inflation is still far too high for the Federal Reserve.

🟢 MICRON REIGNITES THE AI RALLY

Micron reported one of the most remarkable earnings expansions of the AI boom.

Fiscal third-quarter results included:

🔸 Revenue: $41.46 billion

🔸 Year-on-year revenue growth: approximately 346%

🔸 Net income: $28.24 billion

🔸 Adjusted earnings per share: $25.11

🔸 Adjusted gross margin: 84.9%

🔸 Adjusted free cash flow: $18.3 billion

For comparison, Micron generated only $9.3 billion of revenue and $1.9 billion of net income during the same quarter last year.

The company expects the acceleration to continue.

Its fourth-quarter guidance included:

🔸 Revenue of approximately $50 billion

🔸 Adjusted gross margin of roughly 86%

🔸 Adjusted earnings of approximately $31 per share

Micron shares surged approximately 17%–18% before the opening bell.

Why does this matter?

Micron manufactures memory chips.

These chips work alongside processors from companies such as Nvidia and AMD, allowing AI systems to store and rapidly access enormous quantities of data.

Think of an AI processor as a brilliant chef.

The memory system is the kitchen counter.

The chef may be exceptionally fast, but without enough working space, the entire kitchen slows down.

AI companies are therefore rushing to secure advanced memory—and Micron is benefiting from an extraordinary shortage.

🟢 THE MEMORY SHORTAGE MAY LAST BEYOND 2027

Micron’s management expects tight memory-market conditions to persist beyond 2027.

The company has secured 16 long-term strategic agreements with major customers, some lasting between three and five years.

Several customers are even providing billions of dollars in advance payments to secure future supplies.

That matters because memory chips have historically experienced violent boom-and-bust cycles.

Manufacturers expand production when prices are high.

Too much capacity eventually appears.

Prices then collapse.

Micron’s long-term agreements may make this cycle more durable by locking in customer demand before new factories are completed.

Susquehanna estimates that Micron’s annual free cash flow could rise from approximately $49.5 billion during the current fiscal year to more than $110 billion during the following year.

That is an analyst forecast—not company guidance.

But it explains why investors increasingly view Micron as more than a temporary beneficiary of high memory prices.

The market is beginning to ask whether Micron has become a genuine long-term cash-generation machine.

🟢 GLOBAL CHIP STOCKS SURGE

Micron’s results quickly spread across global markets.

Japan’s Nikkei 225 rose approximately 4.6%.

🔸 Kioxia gained roughly 12%

South Korea’s Kospi climbed approximately 5.4%, reversing much of Tuesday’s severe semiconductor sell-off.

🔸 SK Hynix rose approximately 13%

🔸 Samsung Electronics also advanced strongly

In US premarket trading:

🔸 Qualcomm: approximately +12%

🔸 Sandisk: approximately +13%

🔸 Western Digital: more than +12%

The message from investors was clear:

The AI trade still has strong fundamental support.

That does not mean technology valuations are cheap.

It means the demand behind the AI infrastructure boom remains real.

A speculative bubble can rise on excitement alone.

Micron’s results show that at least some companies are now converting that excitement into extraordinary revenue, margins and cash flow.

🟢 SK HYNIX PREPARES FOR A HISTORIC US LISTING

SK Hynix shares received support from two separate developments.

First, Micron’s results showed that global demand for memory chips remains exceptionally strong.

Second, SK Hynix announced plans to list American depositary receipts on Nasdaq.

In simple terms, this would allow US investors to buy SK Hynix shares more easily through the American stock market.

The company reportedly intends to:

🔸 Begin Nasdaq trading on 10 July

🔸 Raise approximately $29 billion–$30 billion

🔸 Broaden access to US and international investors

The transaction could become one of the largest international share offerings in history.

For Micron, this creates both an opportunity and a risk.

Greater attention on SK Hynix could attract more capital towards the entire memory-chip sector.

But it would also give American investors easier access to one of Micron’s largest competitors.

Think of Micron as the main memory-chip shop on a busy American street.

A Nasdaq listing would allow SK Hynix to open a large shop next door.

That could bring more customers to the area—but it would also increase competition for their money.

🟢 US INFLATION RISES ABOVE 4%

The Federal Reserve’s preferred inflation gauge accelerated again in May.

HEADLINE PCE

🔸 Monthly: +0.4%

🔸 Forecast: +0.5%

🔸 Annual: +4.1%

🔸 Previous annual rate: +3.8%

CORE PCE

🔸 Monthly: +0.3%

🔸 Annual: +3.4%

🔸 Previous annual rate: +3.3%

Headline inflation has now exceeded 4% for the first time since April 2023.

The good news was that the monthly headline increase was slightly softer than the 0.5% economists expected.

The bad news was that underlying inflation continued rising.

Core PCE excludes volatile food and energy prices and provides a clearer view of persistent inflation.

Its increase to 3.4% shows that the problem is not confined entirely to oil.

Simple explanation:

The inflation report was not disastrous—but it was not comfortable either.

The monthly headline figure was slightly better than feared.

But the annual figures show that prices are still rising at more than twice the Federal Reserve’s 2% target.

🟢 WHY MARKETS DID NOT PANIC

Despite inflation reaching 4.1%, US stock futures extended their gains after the release.

Shortly after the data:

🔸 Nasdaq 100 futures: approximately +2.3%

🔸 S&P 500 futures: approximately +0.8%

🔸 Dow futures: approximately +0.3%

🔸 US Dollar Index: broadly unchanged near 101.6

There are three reasons markets remained calm.

First, monthly headline inflation was slightly softer than expected.

Second, much of May’s increase reflected the earlier oil shock caused by the Iran war.

Oil prices have since fallen dramatically.

Third, Micron’s earnings provided powerful evidence that AI spending is producing genuine profits.

The market therefore interpreted the data as:

“Inflation is high, but it was not worse than feared—and the AI earnings story remains exceptionally strong.”

🟢 THE REPORT DOES NOT REMOVE FED RATE-RISE RISK

Investors should not mistake a positive stock-market reaction for a dovish inflation report.

Core inflation remains at 3.4%.

Consumer spending is strong.

Income growth is strong.

Business investment is resilient.

Jobless claims remain low.

GDP was revised higher.

That combination gives the Federal Reserve little urgency to reduce interest rates.

It may also leave the door open for another rate increase if inflation remains elevated.

Micron has strengthened the AI earnings story.

But persistent inflation could still limit how much investors are willing to pay for that growth.

🟢 US CONSUMERS KEEP SPENDING

Personal spending increased 0.7% in May.

Economists expected an increase of approximately 0.5%–0.6%.

Personal income also rose 0.7%, beating expectations of roughly 0.4%–0.5%.

Disposable income increased by the same amount.

The personal saving rate recovered from 2.6% to 3%.

This is broadly positive for economic growth.

Consumers earned more, spent more and saved a slightly larger share of their income.

However, part of the income improvement may have come from temporary agricultural-support payments.

The saving-rate rebound should therefore not automatically be viewed as a lasting improvement in household finances.

For the Federal Reserve, stronger spending creates another complication.

Consumers are absorbing higher prices rather than cutting expenditure sharply.

That keeps economic growth alive—but may also allow companies to continue raising prices.

🟢 GDP IS REVISED HIGHER—BUT THE DETAILS ARE WEAKER

First-quarter US economic growth was revised from 1.6% to 2.1% annualised.

That was considerably stronger than expected.

At first glance, the revision suggests the US economy entered the year with more momentum than previously believed.

But the details were less impressive.

The improvement mainly reflected lower imports, which mechanically increase measured GDP.

Meanwhile:

🔸 Real consumer-spending growth was revised down to only 0.5%

🔸 Final sales to private domestic purchasers rose 1.7%

That measure strips out volatile trade, inventory and government components and provides a cleaner view of underlying domestic demand.

The message is therefore mixed.

Headline GDP was stronger.

But the economy’s internal engine was not as powerful as the headline suggests.

🟢 BUSINESS INVESTMENT LOOKS HEALTHIER THAN THE HEADLINE

Headline durable-goods orders fell 4.5% in May after rising 7.9% previously.

That sounds weak.

But the decline was heavily influenced by volatile transportation and defence orders.

The underlying figures were much stronger:

🔸 Durable goods excluding transportation: +1.3%

🔸 Forecast: +0.6%

🔸 Core capital-goods orders excluding aircraft: +1.6%

🔸 Forecast: +0.6%

Core capital-goods orders are often treated as a measure of business-investment intentions.

Their strong increase suggests companies are continuing to spend on equipment despite higher borrowing costs.

That is positive for industrial companies and economic growth.

But it also reinforces the view that the economy may be too resilient to justify lower interest rates.

🟢 THE LABOUR MARKET REMAINS RESILIENT

Initial jobless claims fell to 215,000.

🔸 Forecast: 225,000

🔸 Previous: 227,000

Fewer new claims suggest companies are still reluctant to dismiss workers.

However, continuing claims increased to 1.821 million, slightly above expectations.

That means fewer people are losing jobs—but those who do lose employment may be taking longer to find another position.

The four-week average of claims also edged higher to 224,250.

The labour market is therefore cooling gradually at the edges.

But there is still no evidence of a serious breakdown.

For the Federal Reserve, that resilience provides room to keep policy restrictive while it fights inflation.

🟢 MAY may STILL REPRESENT PEAK INFLATION

May’s inflation data measure the economy before the latest collapse in oil prices.

The Iran war drove energy and transportation costs sharply higher earlier in the year.

Since then, Brent crude has fallen from approximately $126 at its March peak to around $73.

That means today’s inflation report is partly like looking through the rear-view mirror.

It shows where inflation was before energy prices reversed—not necessarily where headline inflation is heading next.

Cheaper oil should gradually reduce:

🔸 Fuel costs

🔸 Transportation expenses

🔸 Manufacturing costs

🔸 Pressure on household budgets

🔸 Headline inflation

But lower oil cannot solve every inflation problem.

Services inflation remains sticky.

Consumer demand remains resilient.

And the enormous AI infrastructure build-out is creating a new source of price pressure.

🟢 AI MAY BE CREATING A THIRD INFLATION WAVE

The AI boom is not merely a software story.

It requires physical infrastructure:

🔸 Memory chips

🔸 Advanced processors

🔸 Data centres

🔸 Cooling systems

🔸 Electrical equipment

🔸 Fibre-optic networks

🔸 Backup generators

🔸 Construction workers

🔸 Enormous quantities of electricity

Five major hyperscalers—Alphabet, Amazon, Meta, Microsoft and Oracle—are expected to spend approximately $741 billion this year, nearly 75% more than last year.

Estimated AI infrastructure investment through 2032 could reach approximately $8 trillion.

That demand is already pushing prices higher.

🔸 Consumer computer software and accessory prices rose around 15% year-on-year in May.

🔸 Wholesale electronic-component prices increased approximately 27%.

🔸 Wages for electrical and wiring contractors rose 6.5%, compared with 3.6% across the broader private sector.

🔸 Data centres may account for almost half of US electricity-demand growth through 2030.

This creates a paradox.

In the long term, AI could reduce inflation by helping workers and companies produce more with fewer resources.

But in the short term, building the infrastructure may do the opposite.

It is like constructing a railway.

Once completed, the railway makes transportation faster and cheaper.

But during construction, it consumes steel, workers, land and capital—pushing costs higher before the benefits arrive.

AI may eventually become disinflationary.

For now, the infrastructure race is inflationary.

🟢 APPLE RAISES PRICES AS “CHIPFLATION” SPREADS

Apple has increased prices across much of its MacBook and iPad range by approximately 20%.

Examples include:

🔸 512GB MacBook Air: $1,099 → $1,299

🔸 256GB iPad Pro: $999 → $1,199

🔸 Entry-level MacBook Neo: increased by as much as 25% in some reported pricing

Apple has not raised iPhone prices for now.

The company blamed unprecedented increases in memory and storage costs caused by the AI data-centre boom.

Micron, SK Hynix and Samsung are prioritising advanced high-bandwidth memory used by AI systems.

That leaves less manufacturing capacity available for traditional consumer products.

Apple’s price rises are important because they provide real-world evidence of the AI inflation problem.

AI companies are willing to pay enormous premiums for scarce memory.

Consumer-electronics companies must then choose between:

🔸 Accepting lower profit margins

🔸 Raising prices

🔸 Reducing production

Apple has chosen to pass part of the cost to customers.

Micron’s gain is becoming the consumer’s bill.

🟢 QUALCOMM ADDS TO THE AI OPTIMISM

Qualcomm shares jumped approximately 12% after the company raised its revenue guidance and announced a partnership with Meta.

The companies plan to develop central processing units for Meta’s infrastructure.

The announcement broadens Qualcomm’s growth story beyond smartphones and towards data centres.

Together, Micron and Qualcomm strengthened the argument that the AI boom is spreading across a wider semiconductor ecosystem.

🟢 IBM ANNOUNCES A MAJOR CHIP BREAKTHROUGH

IBM unveiled what it described as the world’s first sub-one-nanometre chip technology.

Its new 0.7-nanometre—or seven-angstrom—design uses a three-dimensional “nanostack” architecture.

IBM says the technology could offer:

🔸 Up to 50% higher performance

🔸 Up to 70% greater energy efficiency

🔸 Nearly 100 billion transistors on a fingernail-sized chip

IBM shares rose approximately 5%–6% before the opening.

However, investors should understand what was announced.

This is a major research and manufacturing breakthrough—not a chip entering mass production today.

Commercial production could still take several years.

Its significance is long term:

Smaller and more efficient chips could reduce the electricity required for AI and data centres.

That may eventually help relieve one of the AI boom’s largest economic constraints—power consumption.

🟢 OIL FALLS BACK TOWARDS PREWAR LEVELS

Oil continued its extraordinary reversal.

Early Thursday:

🔸 Brent crude: approximately $73 per barrel

🔸 WTI crude: approximately $69–$70

Brent briefly traded near or below the $72.48 closing level recorded immediately before the Iran war began.

That represents a dramatic decline from its March peak near $126.

Why is oil falling?

Tanker traffic through the Strait of Hormuz is recovering much faster than many traders expected.

At least 20 million barrels reportedly left the strait during one 24-hour period.

Thirty-one tankers exited the Gulf on Wednesday—nearly 50% more than the previous day.

Goldman Sachs estimates Gulf oil exports have recovered to approximately 63% of normal levels.

The market is also anticipating:

🔸 Further reopening of Hormuz

🔸 Restarted Gulf production

🔸 Temporary sanctions relief for Iranian oil

🔸 The release of oil previously trapped inside the Gulf

🔸 Possible short-term oversupply

Falling oil is the strongest argument that May may represent the peak in headline inflation.

🟢 THE OIL DECLINE MAY HAVE GONE TOO FAR

Despite the optimism, the energy situation is not fully normal.

More than one billion barrels were reportedly trapped inside the Gulf during the conflict.

The current export surge partly represents delayed oil suddenly reaching the market—not permanently higher production.

Production facilities still need time to restart.

Shipping routes have not fully normalised.

Global inventories have also been heavily depleted.

Some analysts therefore believe the market has become oversold.

Energy Aspects believes crude could eventually establish a floor closer to $80–$90 after the immediate release of trapped oil passes through the market.

Macquarie is more bearish, forecasting third-quarter averages near:

🔸 Brent: $67

🔸 WTI: $62

The disagreement highlights the central risk.

Oil’s short-term direction is being driven by a wave of delayed supply.

Its medium-term direction will depend on Gulf production, inventory rebuilding and whether the peace agreement survives.

🟢 GOLD AND BITCOIN REMAIN UNDER PRESSURE

Gold remains near or below $4,000 an ounce.

Bitcoin is hovering just above $61,000 after falling to $59000 on Wednesday.

Both assets have been pressured by expectations that interest rates may remain high or rise further.

Gold does not pay interest.

When government-bond yields rise, investors can earn higher returns from safer assets, making gold relatively less attractive.

Bitcoin currently behaves partly as a liquidity-sensitive risk asset.

When monetary policy tightens, speculative capital becomes more expensive and investors often reduce exposure.

Today’s inflation report offered some relief because monthly headline inflation was softer than forecast.

But the strong income, spending, employment and business-investment figures prevent the report from being clearly bullish for either asset.

Strategy shares recovered approximately 3% before the opening after falling 9.4% on Wednesday.

The rebound is modest compared with the strength in semiconductor shares.

For now, AI—not crypto—is absorbing the market’s risk appetite.

🟢 CEREBRAS SHOWS THE OTHER SIDE OF THE AI BOOM

Not every AI company is being rewarded.

Cerebras Systems fell almost 20% after reporting its first quarterly results as a public company.

The company delivered a smaller-than-expected loss but warned that gross margins would decline as it expands a large service contract with OpenAI.

Its shares fell below the $185 IPO price and approximately 41% below the peak reached during its first trading day.

This provides an important contrast with Micron.

The market is no longer rewarding every company merely for having an AI connection.

Investors increasingly want:

🔸 Revenue growth

🔸 Strong margins

🔸 Positive cash flow

🔸 Durable customer demand

🔸 A credible route to profitability

Micron delivered those ingredients.

Cerebras raised concerns about margins.

The AI trade is becoming more selective.

🟢 DEFENCE SPENDING REMAINS STRUCTURALLY STRONG

Lockheed Martin secured a seven-year US government contract worth up to $35 billion for Terminal High Altitude Area Defence interceptor missiles.

The award follows plans to increase Patriot missile production.

The contracts reinforce a broader structural investment theme.

Even if fighting in the Middle East eases, governments are rebuilding missile and air-defence inventories depleted by recent conflicts.

Peace may lower near-term oil prices.

It does not necessarily mean defence spending will fall.

🟢 GLOBAL CENTRAL BANKS REMAIN DIVIDED

The world is not experiencing one uniform interest-rate cycle.

Different economies face very different combinations of inflation and growth.

AUSTRALIA

Australia added 40,300 jobs in May, exceeding expectations of approximately 32,000.

🔸 Unemployment fell from 4.5% to 4.4%

🔸 Full-time employment rose by 5,200

🔸 Part-time employment increased by 35,200

🔸 Underemployment edged up to 5.9%

Underlying inflation reached 3.6% in May, above the Reserve Bank of Australia’s 2%–3% target.

The resilient labour market therefore keeps another Australian rate rise possible.

JAPAN

Bank of Japan board member Naoki Tamura argued that underlying inflation has already reached the BOJ’s 2% target.

He believes rates should move steadily towards a neutral level near 2%, potentially through increases of 0.25 percentage points every few months.

The BOJ raised its policy rate to 1% last week—the highest level in 31 years.

After decades of fighting deflation, Japan is now debating how rapidly to tighten monetary policy without damaging growth.

CANADA

The Bank of Canada kept its policy rate at 2.25%.

Policymakers are trying not to overreact to energy-driven inflation because:

🔸 The economy remains weak

🔸 Labour-market slack persists

🔸 Higher oil costs have not spread widely through other prices

🔸 Economic output contracted during two consecutive quarters

Falling oil further reduces the immediate need for tighter Canadian policy.

🟢 WHAT HAPPENS NEXT

The immediate data risk has passed without derailing the AI rally.

But several catalysts remain today:

🔸 Federal Reserve officials Michelle Bowman, John Williams and Austan Goolsbee are scheduled to speak.

🔸 The US Treasury will auction $44 billion of seven-year notes.

The bond auction matters.

Weak demand would force the Treasury to offer investors higher yields.

That could place renewed pressure on technology shares, gold and Bitcoin—even after Micron’s exceptional earnings.

Fed officials will also help determine whether policymakers interpret today’s data as:

🔸 Evidence that inflation may be peaking

or

🔸 Evidence that strong demand will keep inflation elevated

🟢 WHAT IT MEANS FOR INVESTORS

Today’s market contains four competing forces.

1️⃣ MICRON SUPPORTS THE AI TRADE

Explosive revenue, margins and cash flow show that AI infrastructure demand is genuine.

This supports memory manufacturers, semiconductor-equipment companies and much of the broader AI supply chain.

2️⃣ INFLATION IS HIGH—BUT NOT WORSE THAN FEARED

Headline PCE reached 4.1%, but the monthly increase was slightly below expectations.

That was enough to prevent the data from disrupting the technology rally.

3️⃣ THE ECONOMY REMAINS TOO RESILIENT FOR EASY MONEY

Spending, income, business investment and the labour market were stronger than expected.

The Federal Reserve has little reason to cut rates and may still raise them if core inflation remains sticky.

4️⃣ FALLING OIL MAY BRING FUTURE RELIEF

The rapid reversal in energy prices should weaken headline inflation during the coming months.

But it will not automatically solve services inflation or the new demand pressures created by AI infrastructure.

The market is therefore balancing two truths:

The AI boom is stronger than the bears feared.

But the stronger it becomes, the more pressure it may place on chips, electricity, consumer prices, interest rates and valuations.

📌 THE TAKEAWAY

🔸 Micron has proved that the AI boom is producing extraordinary real-world demand—not merely speculative excitement.

🔸 The PCE report showed that inflation remains dangerously elevated, but it was not sufficiently hot to derail the AI rally.

🔸 Strong spending, income, investment and employment suggest the US economy is not approaching an immediate recession.

🔸 Falling oil should ease future headline inflation, although core pressures remain persistent.

🔸 Apple’s price increases show that the AI infrastructure boom is already passing costs from data centres to ordinary consumers.

This is not a simple “risk-on” signal.

It is a battle between exceptional corporate growth and restrictive monetary conditions.

For now, Micron is winning that battle.

But the next phase of the market will depend on whether AI profits can continue growing faster than inflation, interest rates and investor expectations.

"The upward revision to overall GDP should come with an asterisk since the consumer tends to be a big driver of the economy, and it's not good news that personal consumption was lower than initially expected," Martin said. Its strength translated into positive performance for other chip stocks after two sessions of struggles. Checking the data, Micron's earnings per share of $25.11 easily beat consensus of $20.83 while revenue of $41.46 billion topped consensus of $35.85 billion. Gross margin of 84.9% was up sequentially, and the company cited "rapidly growing demand." For the current quarter, Micron projected earnings per share between $30 and $32, well above consensus of $25.72, and revenue of $49 billion to $51 billion. That topped consensus of $43.58 billion, per FactSet. Though Micron's results revived the chip sector this morning after the "great chip dip" earlier this week, one solid earnings report doesn't necessarily negate factors that drove the recent unwinding of crowded positioning. Concerns remain about returns on massive AI investments, concentration of investor money in chip, hyperscaler, and AI-related names, and the possible slowing of compute demand, said Liz Ann Sonders, chief investment strategist at SCFR.

Fed rate hike not certain: The U.S. dollar index hit new 2026 highs Wednesday, supported by ideas the Federal Reserve could hike rates at least once this year. It's fully priced in by October, and there's a rising implied volatility that one could come in September. That's far from assured. "We are not there yet—we need to see how the inflation outlook evolves over the next few months before we become more certain a hike is likely," Martin said. Last week's Fed statement made it clear that the committee favors its inflation mandate over its labor market mandate right now given recent strength in labor. Fed Chairman Kevin Warsh repeatedly stated that the committee was "unambiguous and unanimous" in its commitment to bringing inflation down. The updated "dot plot" of rate expectations also showed that nine participants now project a hike this year. That' doesn't really match the comments from Fed officials over the last month or so where there didn't seem to be an urgency to hike. Inflation trends may become more favorable with the recent drop in oil. In another development, the Fed's annual bank stress test confirmed banks are well positioned to weather a severe recession and continue lending to households and businesses. 美股三大指数集体上涨 美光科技大涨近20%

6月25日，美股三大指数集体上涨，截至发稿，道指涨0.69%，纳指涨0.62%，标普500指数涨0.68%。
￼
个股方面，美光科技大涨近20%，此前公布“王炸”财报，业绩、指引全面超预期，已有多家机构上调其目标价。
光通信、芯片股全线大涨，闪迪涨超16%，西部数据涨超13%，希捷科技涨逾11%，高通涨超10%，英特尔、ARM涨超6%。

全球要闻
美国5月核心PCE同比上涨3.4% 美联储或在9月评估加息选项
美国商务部25日公布的数据显示，美国5月个人消费支出（PCE）价格指数同比上涨4.1%，高于4月的3.8%，剔除食品和能源价格后，5月核心PCE价格指数同比上涨3.4%。分析认为，美国5月整体PCE数据上行主要受美伊冲突推高国际油价影响，但核心PCE同步走强，显示物价上行压力并非仅由短期能源扰动所致。
9月加息稳了？贝森特接连力挺沃什 华尔街：白宫已“开绿灯”！
近几个月来，外界纷纷预测，除非新任美联储主席凯文·沃什能迅速推动更多降息，否则必将面临美国总统特朗普的雷霆之怒。但目前看来，即便沃什支持加息，特朗普似乎也不会发怒。上周的美联储会议比预期更为鹰派，令市场措手不及。点阵图显示，有9位官员预计今年将加息，其中6位甚至预计会有不止一次加息。
霍尔木兹通行量大幅回升！美能源部长：过去24小时2000万桶原油通过
在美伊达成初步协议以及瑞士会谈取得进展后，油轮正陆续驶出霍尔木兹海峡。据一位美国高官表示，目前经由霍尔木兹海峡的原油运输量已与伊朗战争爆发前水平相近。综合央视新闻等媒体报道，美国能源部长赖特周三表示，过去24小时内共有72艘船舶通过霍尔木兹海峡，运输原油约2000万桶——约占全球原油（每日）消费量的五分之一。
商务部回应中美经贸磋商最新进展
商务部新闻发言人何亚东在25日举行的例行新闻发布会上说，按照中美经贸磋商达成的共识，双方同意成立贸易理事会，并将在理事会项下讨论对等降税等合作。双方经贸团队将就此开展进一步磋商。何亚东说，中美双方在飞机和农产品领域的贸易合作是互利共赢的。双方团队将继续保持沟通，鼓励并指导各自企业加强对接，拓展相关领域贸易合作。
盈利预期上修“史无前例”！小摩上调标普500目标价 但“闪崩”风险犹存
摩根大通周三在一份报告中将标普500指数的年底目标价上调至7800点，理由是人工智能（AI）资本支出热潮以及美伊和平进程取得进展后地缘政治环境的改善，推动该指数进入了“史无前例”的盈利预期上调周期。
欧佩克第二大产油国施压提高配额 否则或效仿阿联酋退出组织
伊拉克最新警告，如果石油输出国组织（OPEC）不同意提高其原油生产配额，该国最终可能考虑退出该组织。伊拉克石油部发言人Salim Al-Rikabi在声明中表示，虽然伊拉克“目前无意退出”，但该国认为欧佩克应提高其产量上限；如果这一诉求得不到满足，“就必须作出是否继续留在欧佩克或退出欧佩克的决定”。

Ukraine’s ongoing strike campaign against Russian refineries is exacerbating broader Russian inflationary pressures and complicating the Kremlin’s efforts to conduct expansionary monetary policy. Bloomberg reported on June 24, citing the Russian Federal Statistics Service, that Russian gasoline prices rose three percent to $0.95 USD per liter between June 16 and 22, the largest weekly increase in at least 20 years.[8] Bloomberg reported that Ukrainian strikes against Russia’s largest fuel plants have driven Russians to panic-buy fuel, causing shortages and rising prices. A source familiar with the data told Bloomberg that Russian gasoline production has fallen 15 percent since June 2025 and nine percent since May 2026, likely due to intensified Ukrainian strikes against Russian oil infrastructure. Russian opposition outlet Verstka reported on June 25 that widespread fuel shortages are affecting all Russian federal subjects (regions) except for Ingushetia, Chechnya, Kalmykia, Chukotka, and the Nenets Autonomous Okrug, though prices have increased nationwide.[9] Bloomberg reported that the inflationary effect of rising gasoline prices is seeping into other sectors due to increased production and transportation costs. Bloomberg reported that the Russian Economy Ministry data indicate that total annual inflation increased from 5.3 to 5.8 percent in June 2026. Economy-wide inflationary pressures may disrupt the Russian Central Bank’s continued reductions to the key interest rate. The Russian Central Bank has lowered its key interest rate nine times since June 2025 from 21 percent to 14.25 percent as Russia has attempted to increase capital available to the Russian defense industrial base (DIB) and maintain the facade of domestic economic stability.[10] Russian Central Bank Governor Elvira Nabiullina acknowledged on June 19 that the spike in domestic gasoline prices has affected June’s inflation rate and may increase inflation expectations, implying that the Central Bank may need to change its calculations about the key interest rate if Ukraine’s strikes against Russian energy infrastructure and inflation persist.[11] The Kremlin has exerted significant pressure on the Russian Central Bank and eroded its independence to lower the key interest rate as it attempts to continue expanding capital availability for the defense industry despite expansionary fiscal policies and gasoline shortages exacerbating Russian inflation.[12] The economic effects of Russia’s gasoline shortage will also likely long outlast the physical gasoline shortages in the event that Russia is able to address the shortages. Russian gasoline shortages began in May 2026 and have already spread to the majority of Russian regions and caused inflation to rise in a matter of weeks.[13] Ukrainian forces will likely continue to intensify their strike campaign against Russia oil infrastructure, exacerbate shortages, and increase inflationary pressure across Russia. Expanded inflationary pressures due to the Russian gasoline supply crisis may increase Central Bank resistance to lowering the key interest rate, complicating the Kremlin’s push for expansionary monetary policies and creating administrative tensions.

The Russian military command likely continues to generate new formations on paper, but it remains unclear how or if Russia will be able to staff these formations to doctrinal endstrength. Ukrainian Commander-in-Chief General Oleksandr Syrskyi stated on June 25 that the Russian military command has “adjusted its plans” and now intends to form an unspecified number of new divisions and five new brigades in 2026.[14] It is unclear whether Syrskyi is referring to an additional division and five new brigades or if he is remarking on the continued development of formations that the Russian military command announced in 2022 as part of broader military reforms. Former Russian Defense Minister Sergei Shoigu announced in December 2022 that the Russian military command intended to form 17 new maneuver divisions, including by expanding five existing naval infantry brigades into five divisions and later announced that Russia intended to stand up these new formations between 2023 and 2026.[15] The Russian military command also re-established the Moscow and Leningrad military districts from the former Western Military District as part of these reforms.[16] The Russian military command has formed and deployed at least 10 new Russian divisions to combat since December 2022, although none of these nominally new formations are operating at their doctrinal endstrength.[17] The Russian military command has formed the 104th Airborne (VDV) Division; transformed previously existing brigades into the 27th, 68th, 69th, and 71st motorized rifle divisions; transformed two existing naval infantry brigades into the 55th and 120th Naval Infantry divisions; and formed the 67th, 70th, and 72nd motorized rifle divisions since December 2022. Ukrainian military observer Kostyantyn Mashovets reported in early 2026 that the Russian military command had only formed four new divisions out of the 17 planned maneuver divisions and up to nine brigades, which Russia had planned to form in 2025.[18] The Russian military command reportedly also plans to reorganize its 810th Separate Naval Infantry Brigade into a naval infantry division, which would be the eleventh new maneuver division that Russia has formed since 2022.[19] The Russian military command is generating new formations nominally and only on paper, as Russian infantry-heavy tactics and economic and recruitment constraints will likely hinder the Russian military command from effectively implementing these reforms and fully staffing new formations as they come online.[20]

French authorities seized a Russian shadow fleet oil tanker on June 25. French President Emmanuel Macron reported that the French Navy intercepted the Russian shadow fleet tanker Deliver on June 25 off the coast of Sicily in violation of maritime law.[21] Automatic identification systems (AIS) data from the Starboard Maritime Intelligence ship-tracking platform indicates that the Deliver was flying under the Cameroonian flag and left the Port of Primorsk on June 4 and turned off its AIS tracking on June 23. European states have been increasingly seizing Russian shadow fleet tankers in recent months in an effort to degrade Russia’s ability to transport and thusly profit from sanctioned oil.[22]

Key Takeaways
US Secretary of State Marco Rubio confirmed that Russia and the United States did not reach any agreement to end Russia’s war in Ukraine during the August 2025 Alaska Summit.
The Russian cognitive warfare narrative aimed at painting the Ukrainian frontlines as collapsing appears to have so far failed to persuade Ukraine’s partners to capitulate to Russia’s demands.
Ukraine’s ongoing strike campaign against Russian refineries is exacerbating broader Russian inflationary pressures and complicating the Kremlin’s efforts to conduct expansionary monetary policy.
The Russian military command likely continues to generate new formations on paper, but it remains unclear how or if Russia will be able to staff these formations to doctrinal endstrength.
French authorities seized a Russian shadow fleet oil tanker on June 25.
Ukrainian forces continued their long-range strike campaign against Russian oil infrastructure within Russia.
Russian forces launched one Iskander-M ballistic missile and 90 drones against Ukraine overnight.

We do not report in detail on Russian war crimes because these activities are well-covered in Western media and do not directly affect the military operations we are assessing and forecasting. We will continue to evaluate and report on the effects of these criminal activities on the Ukrainian military and the Ukrainian population and specifically on combat in Ukrainian urban areas. We utterly condemn Russian violations of the laws of armed conflict and the Geneva Conventions and crimes against humanity even though we do not describe them in these reports. 

Ukrainian Operations in the Russian Federation
Ukrainian forces continued their long-range strike campaign against Russian oil infrastructure. The Ukrainian General Staff reported on June 25 that Ukrainian forces struck the Poltavska Oil Depot in Krasnodar Krai, causing a fire.[23] The Ukrainian General Staff noted that the depot supplies oil to Russian forces in occupied Ukraine. Geolocated footage shows smoke and fires at the oil depot.[24] Krasnodar Krai officials acknowledged that Ukrainian strikes caused a fire at the oil depot.[25] Ukraine’s Security Service (SBU) reported on June 25 that Ukrainian forces struck the Bashneft-Novoil and Bashneft-Ufanaftokhim refineries in Ufa, Bashkortostan Republic (over 1,500 kilometers from the frontline), causing fires to both primary AVT installations.[26] Geolocated footage published on June 25 shows fires at both refineries.[27]

Ukrainian sources provided updated battle damage assessments (BDA) of recent Ukrainian strikes. The Ukrainian General Staff reported on June 25 that Ukraine’s June 22 strike against the Vladimir Space Communications Center in Vladimir Oblast critically damaged the main 25-meter parabolic antenna, a roof antenna, the central part of the main hardware-software complex, and Hardware-Technical Building No. 1.[28] The Ukrainian General Staff reported that Ukraine’s June 24 strike against the Orenburg Gas Processing Plant in Orenburg Oblast damaged four gas processing units and halted production at the facility.

Ukraine’s Main Military Intelligence Directorate’s (GUR) Freedom of Russia Legion (LSR) announced on June 24 that it launched Operation Torch against Russian gas infrastructure. The LSR stated on June 24 that it struck six gas distribution facilities in Moscow and Tver oblasts and significantly damaged energy infrastructure, disrupting the operation of individual facilities.[29] The LSR stated that they have been preparing Operation Torch for several months in an effort to further degrade the Russian energy sector and deplete oil revenues.

Russian Supporting Effort: Northern Axis
Russian objective: Create defensible buffer zones in Sumy Oblast along the international border
Russian forces continued offensive operations in northern Sumy Oblast on June 24 and 25 but did not make confirmed advances.[30] A Russian milblogger claimed that Russian forces advanced south of Sopych, west of Chervona Zorya, and north of Sydorivka (all northwest of Sumy City).[31]

Russian Main Effort: Eastern Ukraine
Russian Subordinate Main Effort #1 – Kharkiv Oblast
Russian objective: Push Ukrainian forces back from the international border to create a defensible buffer zone with Belgorod Oblast and approach to within tube artillery range of Kharkiv City
Russian forces continued offensive operations north and northeast of Kharkiv City on June 24 and 25 but did not advance.[32]

Russian forces continued limited ground activity in the Velykyi Burluk direction on June 25 but did not advance.[33]

Russian forces continued to strike energy infrastructure in Kharkiv Oblast. Geolocated footage published on June 22 shows Russian forces conducting a first-person view (FPV) drone strike against a transformer at a substation near Staryi Saltiv (east of Kharkiv City).[34]

Russian Subordinate Main Effort #2 – Oskil River
Russian objective: Cross the Oskil River in Kharkiv Oblast and push westward into eastern Kharkiv Oblast and northern Donetsk Oblast

Russian forces continued offensive operations in the Kupyansk direction on June 24 and 25 but did not advance.[35]

Russian forces continued limited offensive activity in the Borova direction on June 25 but did not advance.[36]

Ukrainian forces continued their intermediate-range strike campaign against Russian military assets and ground lines of communication (GLOCs) in occupied Luhansk Oblast. The Ukrainian General Staff reported on June 25 that Ukrainian forces struck two rail bridges over the Aidar and Luhanchyk rivers, which Russian forces used to transfer troops and deliver ammunition to frontline positions.[37] The Ukrainian General Staff reported that Ukrainian forces also struck a Russian material and technical warehouse near occupied Novohannivka (roughly 118 kilometers from the frontline).[38]

Russian Subordinate Main Effort #3 – Donetsk Oblast
Russian objective: Capture the entirety of Donetsk Oblast, the claimed territory of Russia’s proxies in Donbas, and advance into Dnipropetrovsk Oblast

Russian forces continued offensive operations in the Slovyansk direction on June 24 and 25 but did not advance.[39] Geolocated footage published on June 25 shows Russian forces striking a Ukrainian position in southern Lyman, in areas where Russian sources previously claimed that Russian forces maintained positions.[40]

Russian forces are conducting glide bomb strikes against Ukrainian positions in the Slovyansk direction. Geolocated footage published on June 25 shows Russian forces using three guided FAB-1500 glide bombs to strike a Ukrainian temporary deployment point located at the Slovyansk Thermal Power Plant in Mykolaivka (east of Slovyansk).[41] The Russian Ministry of Defense (MoD) claimed on June 25 that Russian forces conducted a FAB-500 glide bomb strike against a crossing over the Siverskyi Donets River near Mayaky (north of Slovyansk), dropped six glide bombs on the retreating Ukrainian forces near Shchurove (southwest of Lyman), and used four FAB-500 glide bombs to strike Ukrainian positions in western Lyman.[42] A Russian milblogger published footage on June 24 reportedly showing Russian forces conducting a FAB-1500 glide bomb strike against Ukrainian positions near Vysokoivanivka (east of Slovyansk).[43] These intensified glide bomb strikes are likely part of a Russian battlefield air interdiction (BAI) campaign aimed at setting conditions for offensive operations against Slovyansk and depriving Ukrainian logistics to Lyman.

Russian forces are actively using strike drones to target Ukrainian logistics but are struggling to advance in the Lyman direction. The commander of a Ukrainian battalion operating in the Lyman direction reported on June 25 that the situation in the Lyman direction remains stable and that Russian forces are not making tactical gains.[44] The commander noted that Russian forces continue to actively use strike drones of various types capable of operating at greater depths and reach Ukrainian logistics routes with support from elements of the elite Russian Rubikon Center for Advanced Unmanned Technologies. The commander noted that Russian forces are not actively using heavy bomber drones in the Lyman direction at this time.

Russian forces recently conducted an infiltration mission in the Kostyantynivka-Druzhkivka tactical area. Geolocated footage published on June 17 shows Ukrainian forces striking a Russian position in Kostyantynivka after what ISW assesses was a Russian infiltration mission.[45]

The Kremlin continues to propagate a systematic cognitive warfare effort alleging widespread Russian advances in Kostyantynivka. The Russian Ministry of Defense (MoD) continued on June 25 its recent pattern of publishing hyper-detailed falsified reports about Russian gains in Kostyantynivka in order to aggrandize Russian successes.[46] Kremlin newswire TASS also published an interview on June 25 with the deputy commander of the Russian 1st Battalion of the 1194th Motorized Rifle Regiment (4th Separate Motorized Rifle Brigade, 3rd Combined Arms Army [CAA], formerly 2nd Luhansk People’s Republic Army Corps [LNR AC], Southern Military District [SMD]) describing claimed advances in Kostyantynivka.[47] These continued claims are part of the Kremlin’s cognitive warfare effort aimed at exaggerating Russian advances along the entire frontline, contrary to all available evidence.[48]

Russian forces are increasingly using glide bombs in the Kostyantynivka-Druzhkivka tactical area. A Ukrainian brigade operating in the area reported on June 24 that Russian forces have intensified glide bomb strikes against Druzhkivka in the past month.[49] A Russian milblogger posted footage on June 24 purporting to show Russian forces conducting FAB-1500 and FAB-250 guided glide bomb strikes against Ukrainian positions near Bilenke (north of Druzhkivka).[50]

Russian forces continue attempts to advance toward Dobropillya frompositions north of Pokrovsk. The Ukrainian 7th Rapid Reaction Corps reported on June 24 that Russian forces are attempting to advance toward Dobropillya on two flanks – from Hryshyne (south of Dobropillya) and from Rodynske and Bilytske (both southeast of Dobropillya).[51] The 7th Corps reported that Russian forces use occupied Donetsk City, Horlivka, and Makiivka (all within 40 kilometers of the frontline) as logistics hubs to supply Russian forces operating in the Pokrovsk and Dobropillya directions and Kostyantynivka and Chasiv Yar directions. The 7th Corps reported that Russian forces are using Pokrovsk and Myrnohrad as springboards for accumulating forces and as a platform for deploying drone launch sites, repeaters, and electronic warfare (EW) systems, using urban development areas to camouflage equipment.

Russian forces continued offensive operations in the Pokrovsk direction on June 25 but did not make confirmed advances.[52] A Russian milblogger claimed that Russian forces seized Myrne (northwest of Pokrovsk).[53]

Ukrainian forces are interdicting Russian logistics and communications in the Pokrovsk direction to degrade Russian offensive capabilities. The Ukrainian 7th Rapid Reaction Corps reported on June 25 that Russian forces continue to concentrate forces and resources in Pokrovsk in an attempt to use Pokrovsk as a logistics hub but that Ukrainian forces are hampering Russian attempts to advance by striking their communication systems and drone launch points.[54] The 7th Corps noted that periodic Russian communication losses result in drone control disruptions, meaning that Russian forces have fewer drones operating at a given time, reducing Russia’s ability to advance.

Russian forces are reportedly using electric motorized vehicles for offensive operations in the Pokrovsk direction as gasoline shortages in Russia are intensifying. The spokesperson of a Ukrainian brigade operating in the Pokrovsk direction reported on June 24 that Russian forces started using silent, likely electric, motorcycles and scooters to approach Ukrainian positions, likely due to ongoing Russian fuel shortages.[55]

Russian forces continued limited offensive operations in the Novopavlivka direction on June 24 and 25 but did not advance.[56]

Russian forces recently conducted an infiltration mission in the Oleksandrivka direction. Geolocated footage published on June 25 shows Ukrainian forces striking a Russian position in western Ternove (southeast of Oleksandrivka) after what ISW assesses was a Russian infiltration mission.[57]

Russian forces are attempting to regain previously-held positions in the Oleksandrivka direction as Ukrainian strikes against Russian logistics are curtailing Russian offensive capabilities. Ukrainian Southern Defense Forces Spokesperson Colonel Vladyslav Voloshyn reported on June 25 that Russian forces are intensifying artillery strikes in order to hinder Ukrainian counterattacks and regain lost positions in the Oleksandrivka direction.[58] The commander of a Ukrainian battalion operating in the Oleksandrivka direction reported on June 25 that Ukrainian offensive actions and strikes against Russian logistics are inhibiting Russian combat capabilities and forcing them to operate on a defensive footing.[59]

Ukrainian forces continued their intermediate-range strike campaign against Russian military assets in occupied Donetsk Oblast on June 24 and 25. Geolocated footage published on June 24 shows Ukrainian forces striking a Russian Shahed drone launch vehicle at the occupied Donetsk City International Airport (roughly 48 kilometers from the frontline).[60] The Ukrainian General Staff reported on June 25 that Ukrainian forces struck a Russian command post near occupied Tsukuryne (roughly 30 kilometers from the frontline).[61] Donetsk People’s Republic (DNR) Head Denis Pushilin claimed on June 24 that Ukrainian forces struck a bus, engineering equipment, and eight trucks in occupied Donetsk Oblast.[62]
